data.title: Texas Capital projects mid- to high single-digit revenue growth and $265M–$290M noninterest income for 2026 while expanding fee income streams
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Capital Bancshares (TCBI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Holmes described 2025 as a “defining year” for Texas Capital, emphasizing the completion of the firm’s transformation and the achievement of key targets: “In the third quarter, we achieved our stated financial targets, marking completion of our transformation and delivering the largest organic profitability improvement of any commercial bank exceeding $20 billion in assets over the past 2 decades.” Holmes highlighted a 1.2% ROAA in Q4 and noted that “full year adjusted ROAA of 1.04% represents a 30 basis point improvement versus 2024 and signals a fundamental improvement of our earnings power.”
  • Holmes reported record adjusted total revenue of $1.3 billion, adjusted net income to common stockholders of $314 million, adjusted EPS of $6.80, and adjusted pre-provision net revenue of $489 million. He added, “We achieved record tangible common equity to tangible assets of 10.56% and record tangible book value per share of $75.25.”
  • The CEO also stated, “Fee income areas of focus generated $192 million in 2025 with substantial growth opportunity ahead.” He pointed out that the business is now positioned for “consistent execution and compounding returns,” driven by balance sheet growth, operating leverage, and expanding fee income.
  • CFO Matt Scurlock stated, “Fourth quarter results capped a record year with broad-based improvements across all key metrics. Our increasingly durable business model, uniquely positioned to deliver high-quality client outcomes, is translating into sustainably strong financial performance.” Scurlock highlighted a year-over-year quarterly revenue increase of 15% to $327.5 million and record full year net interest income of $1.03 billion. He noted, “Quarterly adjusted noninterest expense decreased 2% or $4.2 million to $186.4 million, benefiting from continued expense realignment.”

Outlook

  • Management projected mid- to high single-digit total revenue growth for 2026, driven by client adoption and fee income expansion. Scurlock stated, “We anticipate total revenue growth in the mid- to high single-digit range, driven by industry-leading client adoption and continued growth in our fee income areas of focus with full year noninterest revenue expected to reach $265 million to $290 million.”
  • Expected noninterest expense growth is in the mid-single digits, reflecting increased compensation and targeted expansion. Provision outlook for 2026 is 35 to 40 basis points of average loans held for investment (excluding mortgage finance), moderated due to economic uncertainty.
  • Management’s language points to disciplined execution and a focus on scaling investments to further drive profitability.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income to common was $94.6 million for the quarter, or $2.08 per share, up 45% year-over-year. Full year adjusted net income to common was $313.8 million or $6.80 per share, a 53% improvement over 2024.
  • Full year adjusted total revenue reached $1.26 billion, up 13% year-over-year, with net interest income growing 14% to $1.03 billion. Adjusted fee-based revenue grew 9% to $229 million, a third consecutive year of record fee income.
  • Commercial loan balances expanded $254 million or 8% annualized during Q4; gross loans held for investment increased $1.6 billion or 7% year-over-year to $24.1 billion, mainly from commercial loans.
  • The allowance for credit loss including off-balance sheet reserves stood at $333 million, near all-time highs, with the credit loss ratio at 1.82% of total loans held for investment (excluding mortgage finance).
  • Tangible common equity to tangible assets finished at 10.6%, with tangible book value per share increasing 13.44% year-over-year to $75.25.

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked about the investment banking pipeline and fee pacing for 2026. Holmes replied, “IV transaction volume was up about 40%. The fees were much more granular. So people like you and others would suggest that's healthy, better earnings stream.” Scurlock guided investment banking fees of $160 million to $175 million for 2026 and total noninterest income of $265 million to $290 million.
  • Lay also asked about capital deployment and buybacks. Scurlock responded, “Pushing CET1 up 75 basis points to 12.13%, while growing loans, $1.6 billion or 7%, buying back 5% of the company for 114% of prior month tangible, and building tangible book value per share by 13.44%. We’re obviously pretty pleased with how we utilize shareholders' capital for their benefit in 2025.”
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about expense outlook and hiring. Scurlock said, “We are highly focused on leveraging the material previous investments that we've made by expanding capabilities and adding targeted coverage with the 2026 expense guide, continue to heavily feature growth in salaries and benefits with select increase in technology.”
  • Jackson Singleton, Autonomous: Asked about credit trends and provision guidance. Scurlock explained, “We did experience modest linked quarter increase in special mentioned loans, which…was tied exclusively to a handful of multifamily properties that are experiencing net operating income pressure, just given required rental concessions to maintain target occupancy levels.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on growth sustainability, capital allocation, credit trends, and margin guidance, with a generally positive but probing tone focused on underlying risk and earnings durability.
  • Management’s sentiment was confident during prepared remarks, with statements like “our strategy, platform, talent and momentum position us to achieve these objectives.” During Q&A, responses remained detailed and constructive, but showed caution on economic uncertainty and credit outlook, as in “We did experience modest linked quarter increase in special mentioned loans…We still felt it prudent to move to 35% to 40%.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a slightly more positive tone, probing the durability of growth and capital deployment, while management’s remarks shifted from transformation milestones to execution and scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from “low double-digit percent growth” for full year 2025 revenue in Q3 to “mid- to high single-digit range” for 2026.
  • Management focus moved from concluding a multi-year transformation to emphasizing execution, scale, and realization of investments.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on loan growth, fee income, credit quality, and capital strategy, but current questions leaned slightly more into the ongoing scalability and risk management aspects.
  • The tone of management has remained confident, but the language indicates a pivot to operational discipline rather than strategic overhaul.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing economic uncertainty as a reason for moderating the 2026 provision outlook.
  • Credit trends, particularly in multifamily commercial real estate, were highlighted as a concern, with rental concessions affecting net operating income.
  • Analysts raised questions about the sustainability of fee growth, capital deployment amid market shifts, and credit risk exposure in select portfolios.

Final Takeaway

Texas Capital concluded 2025 with record results across revenue, net income, and fee income, underscoring the success of its multi-year transformation. Management projects continued positive operating leverage and meaningful earnings growth for 2026, with a focus on mid- to high single-digit revenue growth and expanding fee income streams. The company is emphasizing disciplined execution, operational scale, and risk management as it seeks to sustain industry-leading returns and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump accuses JPMorgan of political debanking in $5B lawsuit - report
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday is filing a $5B lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and its longtime CEO Jamie Dimon, according to a media report.

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.

data.title: Mortgage rates edge up but well below year-ago level
data.description:
Mortgage rates inched up this week but remained well below the year-ago level, according to the latest Freddie Mac (FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.09% as of January 22, up slightly from 6.06% last week but below 6.96% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.44%, up from 5.38% a week ago but down from the year-ago 6.16%.

"With the economy improving and the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage nearly a percentage point lower than last year, more homebuyers are entering the market," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

Here is a look at the mortgage rate movements in the last one year:

data.title: Old Second signals mid-single-digit loan growth target for 2026 while maintaining margin above 5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old Second Bancorp, Inc. (OSBC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • James Eccher, President, CEO & Chairman of the Board, reported "net income was $28.8 million or $0.54 per diluted share in the fourth quarter, and ROA was 1.64%." He noted that "fourth quarter earnings were impacted by a couple of material adjusting items, the first being a $428,000 pretax loss on mortgage servicing rights and a $2.5 million in pretax acquisition-related expenses driven by $1.5 million of computer and data processing related to the core systems conversion, as well as systems related to acquired operations." Excluding these, net income was $30.8 million or $0.58 per diluted share. Eccher highlighted a "tangible book value per share increased 61 basis points to $14.12" and a "tangible equity ratio increased 61 basis points from last quarter from 10.41% to 11.02%." He added "our financials continue to reflect an exceptionally strong net interest margin at 5.09% for the fourth quarter, which is a 4 basis point improvement from last quarter." He discussed a "slight decrease in total loans of $12.4 million from last quarter" and an increase in nonperforming loans and classified assets, with net loan charge-offs of $6 million, mainly from the Powersport portfolio and commercial real estate owner occupied. Eccher stated, "the contribution margin is now at a multiyear high in this business, and we're very bullish on our 2026 performance."
  • Bradley Adams, Executive VP, COO & CFO, said, "we're pretty darn excited to close the year like this. Running at a north of a 5% margin and ROA handsomely above 1.5% and ROTCE above 17.5% on an operating basis is pretty exceptional performance that we're proud of." He indicated "net interest income increased nominally this quarter relative to last quarter both around the $83 million level." Adams commented on loan growth targeting "the mid-single-digit level for next year" and expense growth will be modest. He mentioned the buyback is "on the table that we haven't done anything this quarter. It's becoming inevitable."

Outlook

  • Adams suggested "loan growth being targeted in the mid-single-digit level for next year. Expense growth will be modest. Pre-inflationary trends in employee benefits and salaries are going to be moderated by the realization of the cost saves associated with Evergreen."
  • Adams stated, "we may tick down modestly in the first quarter, but I expect to still be above 5%." He also said, "I'd be very surprised if we're not around the 5% level for the full year 2027."
  • Eccher expressed optimism, saying, "we're optimistic about next year or this year and all the opportunities that are in front of Old Second."

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $28.8 million or $0.54 per diluted share, with an adjusted net income of $30.8 million or $0.58 per diluted share excluding notable items. The tangible book value per share rose to $14.12. The net interest margin was 5.09%. The tangible equity ratio increased to 11.02%. Common equity Tier 1 was 12.99%. The loan-to-deposit ratio reached 93.9%. The allowance for credit losses on loans was $72.3 million or 1.38% of total loans, down from $75 million or 1.43% the previous quarter.
  • There was a decrease in total loans of $12.4 million from last quarter, and noninterest income reflected a slight decrease compared to the prior quarter, but increased $544,000 compared to the prior year like quarter. Noninterest expenses declined $10.2 million from the previous quarter.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co.: Asked about cost savings and expense outlook. Adams responded, "There's a tailwind to '26...We've done a lot of things to restructure to keep those costs contained. But we've got a couple of branch closings that are scheduled and some other expense initiatives...it kind of feels like a 3% type level as we get those final cost saves run through."
  • Rulis: Inquired on net charge-off pace in Powersports. Eccher said, "I think we need to be accustomed to a little bit higher net charge-off rate due to Powersports...we expect Powersports to have maybe elevated charge-offs in the next couple of quarters...the contribution margin, which I mentioned was at a multiyear high."
  • Adam Kroll: Asked about margin expectations. Adams replied, "I'd be very surprised if we're not around the 5% level for the full year 2027."
  • Kroll: Questioned about deposit runoff. Adams explained, "We probably need to replace $300 million to $400 million in deposits with our type of funding in order to complete the process."
  • Kroll: Queried on Powersports loan growth. An executive responded, "Slightly less than that would be my expectation."
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens: Sought clarity on Powersport borrower profile and seasonality. Darin Campbell, Director, EVP & President, National Specialty Lending, stated, "the average FICO score for our portfolio in the Powersports and 730...from a seasonality perspective...our busy season starts March 1 through -- it's really the second and the third quarter from an origination perspective."
  • McEvoy: Asked about share repurchase and M&A. Adams said, "M&A market feels good. There's no shortage of discussions happening. The question is what's the right deal for Old Second at the right time and how much capital do we need to do that."
  • Brian Martin, Janney: Inquired about loan production and pipeline. Eccher replied, "The fourth quarter actually, surprisingly, was our best production quarter of the year...the pipeline today or as of the end of the year is the highest it's been in probably 6 to 7 quarters."
  • Martin: Asked about expense growth. Adams confirmed, "No, that's what I'm talking about" regarding 3% year-over-year expense growth.
  • Martin: Questioned on buyback timing. Adams said, "I expect it will begin in relatively short order, yes."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cost saves, loan growth, Powersports credit quality, and capital management, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, probing for clarity on expense containment and margin durability.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, particularly regarding loan growth, cost saves, and margin resilience. Adams described the company's performance as "pretty exceptional," while Eccher was "very bullish on our 2026 performance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a similarly constructive but probing tone, while management’s confidence appeared even stronger, with repeated emphasis on strategic momentum and margin strength. Management’s tone during prepared remarks remained upbeat, and during Q&A, sustained optimism and directness in responses.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted to a clear target of mid-single-digit loan growth for the next year, compared to the previous quarter's low to mid-single-digit guide. Margin outlook remains firm above 5%, with only a modest potential tick down expected in the first quarter. Expense growth expectations moderated from 4% (excluding Evergreen) to 3% including cost saves. The buyback moved from being "on the table" to "inevitable" and expected to begin soon. The integration of Evergreen is now described as fully completed, with focus shifting to organic growth and balance sheet optimization. Analysts continued to focus on credit quality in Powersports, loan growth, and deposit funding, while management’s confidence in margin stability and cost controls strengthened.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted higher-than-expected net charge-offs in the Powersport portfolio, though contribution margins remain strong. There are slight increases in nonperforming loans and classified assets. The company anticipates continued elevated charge-offs in Powersports through the next few quarters due to the higher interest rate environment. Asset quality in C&I and commercial real estate is under ongoing review, and management is monitoring economic headwinds reflected in classified assets and loan charge-offs.

Final Takeaway

Old Second Bancorp closed the year with strong financial results, highlighted by a net interest margin above 5%, robust loan production, and a completed integration of Evergreen Bank. Management targets mid-single-digit loan growth for 2026, expects margin stability, and plans to initiate share repurchases in the near term. Powersports remains a high-margin, higher-loss portfolio, but the contribution margin is at a multiyear high and supports ongoing profitability. Expense growth is projected to be modest, supported by cost saves. The company expresses confidence in its balance sheet positioning and its outlook for the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Winter storm watch: Costco is set to benefit from the pantry-loading phenomenon
data.description:
Winter Storm Fern is forecast to become a major, long-duration winter storm affecting the South, Midwest, and Northeast parts of the U.S. from Friday through Monday. The system will bring a corridor of heavy snow and a significant, potentially damaging ice storm, with some areas facing near-impossible travel and widespread power outages. The impact of the storm could reach half the U.S. population, according to The Weather Channel.

The forecast calls for snow, sleet, and freezing rain to develop Friday from the southern Rockies and Plains into Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and parts of the Mid-South, then spread east into the Tennessee Valley, Appalachians, and Carolinas through Saturday and Saturday night. By Sunday, the storm will shift into the Ohio Valley, Mid-Atlantic, and Northeast, with increasing odds for heavy snow along the Interstate 95 corridor from Washington, D.C. to Boston and lingering impacts into early Monday.

Mizuho Securities analyst David Bellinger said the firm expects Costco (COST) to benefit in the near term from the well-publicized storm. He noted that Costco (COST) has typically seen a surge in sales and traffic as large-scale events approach and as consumers pantry load in preparation. For investors, the pantry-loading effect for Costco (COST) is considered likely to help the U.S. business put up a strong sales growth tally compared to a year ago when a polar vortex also drove traffic. The two-year Costco (COST) comparable sales mark could be even stronger due to mild weather for key parts of the U.S. in January 2024.

Anecdotal posts on social media sites show Costco (COST) stores are crowded in states such as Texas, Georgia, Tennessee, and Florida.

Shares of Costco (COST) are up more than 12% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Franklin Templeton launches new ETF focused on emerging market debt
data.description:
Franklin Templeton (BEN) is debuting a new actively managed exchange-traded fund focused on emerging market debt, it said on Thursday.

The Templeton Emerging Markets Debt ETF (NYSE Arca: TEMD) offers an emerging markets debt strategy positioned between fully U.S. dollar–denominated and fully local currency–denominated approaches. 

Under normal market conditions, the fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in emerging market debt securities and in derivatives and other instruments that provide similar investment exposure.

The fund also invests at least 50% of its net assets in U.S. dollar-denominated and euro-denominated debt securities issued by emerging market countries included in the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index.

The launch comes at a time when investors have been increasingly shifting capital into foreign and emerging markets, driven in part by stretched U.S. valuations and a weaker dollar narrative. At press time, the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) gained 7.6% year-to-date and nearly 40% Y/Y, while the S&P 500 was roughly flat YTD and rose 14% from a year earlier.

data.title: Horizon Bancorp outlines 2026 net interest margin guidance of 4.25%–4.35% amid commercial lending focus and margin expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Horizon Bancorp, Inc. (HBNC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Prame stated that "Horizon’s fourth quarter results demonstrate the core strength of our community banking model and the excellent execution of the balance sheet repositioning." He highlighted that the quarter exceeded prior performance estimates, with annualized return on average assets above 1.6%, return on average equity approaching 16%, and a net interest margin of 4.29%.
  • Prame emphasized ongoing loan growth and credit quality, noting, "the team performed well, strategically reducing our portfolio of higher cost transactional deposits." He added, "Fee income continued to make progress, and our expense management efforts reflect our commitment to continually improve our operating leverage."
  • Executive VP & Chief Commercial Banking Officer Lynn Kerber reported, "Total loans were $4.9 billion at December 31, an increase of $60.7 million from September 30." She cited commercial loans as the lead strategy, with a $76 million increase in Q4, representing 9% growth on an annualized basis.
  • Kerber discussed credit quality, stating, "Substandard loans of $59.4 million represent 1.22% of loans for the fourth quarter, a decrease from 1.31% for the third quarter. Non-performing loans of $34.9 million represent 72 basis points of loans for the fourth quarter, an increase from 64 basis points in the third quarter."
  • CFO John Stewart stated, "Q4 marks the ninth consecutive quarter of net interest margin expansion, totaling 188 basis points from the low in Q3 of 2023." He added, "the net interest margin increased by 77 basis points to 4.29% and above the upper end of our guidance range."

Outlook

  • Stewart shared, "we have increased our net interest margin outlook for the full year 2026, which we now expect to be in the range of 4.25% to 4.35%."
  • He projected period-end loans and deposit balances to grow mid-single digits, with deposit balances expected to grow modestly more than loan balances.
  • Stewart indicated, "Non-FTE net interest income is now expected to grow in the low teens year-over-year."
  • The effective tax rate is anticipated in the range of 18% to 20%. Fee income is expected in the mid-$40 million range and expenses in the mid-$160 million range.

Financial Results

  • Prame stated, "annualized return on average assets above 1.6%, return on average equity approaching 16% and a net interest margin of 4.29%."
  • Kerber reported commercial loans increased by $76 million in Q4, with growth balanced across key markets in Michigan and Indiana.
  • Kerber noted, "Net charge-offs were $1 million in the quarter, representing 8 basis points on an annualized basis."
  • Stewart confirmed, "reported noninterest income results were broadly in line with expectations at $11.5 million for the quarter. Excluding securities losses in the comparable period, total fee income was up 7% year-over-year."
  • Stewart said, "at $40.6 million, expenses were generally in line with expectations and as planned, included $0.7 million related to the write-off of the remaining unamortized issuance expense for the subordinated notes we called on October 1."

Q&A

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about upside and downside risks for 2026. Stewart replied the outlook is slightly more favorable, specifically in NII, and that "the ability to grow organic core deposits to fund organic core commercial loan growth" is the key lever for upside.
  • Nosal asked if consumer loan declines could ease. Prame answered, "our consumer loan portfolio right now is primarily made up of HELOCs and consumer closed-end mortgages that deal with real estate... we're not seeing the consumer side to be something that we're going to probably push to accelerate."
  • Nosal questioned asset quality and NPAs. Kerber responded, "substandard loans have decreased for the last 3 quarters and roughly 8% for 2025... our overall metrics are good."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Inquired about margin drivers. Stewart explained, "New origination yields continue to sit on a coupon basis above 6.5%. We've got cash flows coming off the portfolio on the loan side that are still below 6%."
  • Race asked about capital deployment. Prame stated, "there's ample opportunity internally to grow and expand our organic business model... this is going to continue to be our primary focus."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about fee income drivers. Stewart replied, "there's not one piece of that fee business that is towing the line, so to speak, modest kind of low- to mid uptick in service charges and interchange would be the assumption."
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens: Asked if commercial loan growth would be weighted towards CRE. Kerber said, "our mix hasn't changed much over the last 3 years... Our equipment finance division has been a very nice complementary piece."
  • Brian Martin, Janney: Asked about loan and deposit pricing. Kerber said, "it really just depends on the segment of the customer and their profile."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, seeking clarity on growth drivers, margin sustainability, and capital deployment while probing for potential risks.
  • Management maintained a confident and slightly optimistic tone, with Stewart stating, "we have increased our net interest margin outlook," and Prame highlighting, "we're very well positioned entering 2026 to create significant shareholder value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A was more optimistic, with analysts continuing their focus on growth and risk but with less skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net interest margin was increased for 2026, now expected in the range of 4.25% to 4.35%, compared to 4.2% to 4.3% previously.
  • Commercial loan growth continued as the primary focus, with ongoing strategic reduction of high-cost transactional deposits.
  • Analysts shifted focus from post-restructuring stability in Q3 to margin outlook, growth levers, and capital deployment in Q4.
  • Management confidence increased, as reflected in more favorable outlook language compared to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the need for continued growth in organic core deposits to fund loan expansion as a key variable.
  • Kerber noted a modest increase in non-performing loans but emphasized overall credit quality remains strong.
  • Stewart acknowledged a potential modest increase in expenses but described it as not material.

Final Takeaway

Horizon Bancorp’s leadership underscored the completion of its balance sheet repositioning and reported strong Q4 performance, with commercial lending and margin expansion as major drivers. Management’s updated 2026 outlook projects further net interest margin improvement and mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth, supported by a continued focus on disciplined operating execution and credit quality. The company positions itself for steady, durable profitability and capital generation going forward.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Live Oak signals momentum with 17% loan growth and targets $1B Live Oak Express production over time
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. (LOB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • William C. (BJ) Losch III, President, highlighted that "2025 was quite an interesting year," noting persistent macro uncertainty, rate decreases from the Fed, and a successful preferred offering. He emphasized, "record loan production, 17% loan growth, 27% core PPNR growth, 17% revenue growth and 13% tangible book value growth" as well as accelerated momentum in Live Oak Express and Checking initiatives.

  • Losch stated that over the last two years, loan production grew 57% and pipelines remain strong for 2026. He underscored, "adjusted PPNR up 27% over 2024 and adjusted EPS up 49%." The company increased customers with both loan and deposit relationships from roughly 6% to 22% in 2025.

  • Walter Phifer, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "Q4 producing $44 million of net income and $0.95 of earnings per share, both of which were approximately 3x Q4 of 2024." He pointed out Q4's loan production of $1.6 billion, capping off a record $6.2 billion year. Phifer added, "Business checking balances of $377 million doubled year-over-year," and Live Oak Express contributed $12 million in gain on sale totals for 2025.

Outlook

  • Phifer explained, "Our base outlook for the Fed consists of 3 Fed cuts in March, June and September of 2026. Any less cuts or cuts later in the year will provide an earnings opportunity for the bank." Management expects stable or low rate environments and continued strong loan growth to support growth, margin, and credit outlook.

  • Strategic focus will remain on expanding business checking and Live Oak Express, with ongoing investment in technology and AI to enhance efficiency and customer service.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $44 million and earnings per share were $0.95, both about three times higher than Q4 2024.

  • Q4 loan production reached $1.6 billion, supporting 17% annual loan balance growth for the year. Year-over-year customer deposit growth was 18%, while business checking balances increased to $377 million. Net interest income increased by $8 million or 7% quarter-over-quarter, and by $26 million or 26% year-over-year.

  • Gain on sale was lower in Q4 due to large investment gains allowing loan sale flexibility, resulting in an increase of loans held for sale by $60 million quarter-over-quarter.

  • Nonaccrual loans increased to $110 million, predominantly SBA credits, but management cited alignment with broader industry trends.

Q&A

  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler & Co.: Asked about NII and NIM trajectory into Q1. Phifer responded, "Typically, any time you see 50 basis points of Fed cuts... we have a large variable quarterly adjusted loan portfolio that reprices on the first business day -- so that will drive both NIM and net interest income compression in the near term."

  • Love questioned gain on sale income trends. Phifer said, "I think Q1, historically for us is our lowest quarter of the year... Q1 2026 will be closer to what you see Q1 2025, so it will be a step up versus what you saw on Q4."

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Probed margin trajectory with three embedded Fed cuts. Phifer replied, "Stable environments work really well for us... We'll expect that step down here in Q1. And then our expectation is to go back on that, start seeing the up and right trajectory or NIM expansion as we move through the year."

  • Feaster inquired about expense trends. Phifer indicated expense growth will likely moderate to single digits year-over-year, focusing investments on business checking and Live Oak Express.

  • Feaster also asked about credit trends. Michael Cairns, Chief Credit Officer, stated, "past dues are low... classified loans are flat to slightly improving over the quarter," and highlighted that the quarter was "fairly uneventful and stable."

  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Clarified margin compression magnitude and other loan income. Phifer explained nonrecurring high prepayment penalties accounted for the uptick, not expected to recur.

  • Rochester asked about Live Oak Express growth. Losch said, "our aspirational goals are $1 billion a year of production at Cruise altitude. That's not next year. That's over time."

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Sought clarity on SOP changes in small dollar loans and competitive impacts. Losch noted, "We've started to see some lenders back away... we're going to be choosier about who we do business with," but expects Live Oak's ease of doing business to differentiate the bank.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on granular operational details, margin trajectory, and strategic initiatives.

  • Management tone was confident and optimistic in prepared remarks, shifting to measured and detailed in Q&A, using phrases like "we are very optimistic about the opportunity in front of us in 2026 and beyond."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed consistent confidence, with more focus on execution and operational resilience.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained optimistic, but management now projects three Fed cuts in 2026 and highlights more deliberate expense management.

  • Strategic focus shifted to accelerating business checking and Live Oak Express, with an explicit aspirational target of $1 billion annual production for Live Oak Express.

  • Analysts' questions were more specific regarding margin, loan sale timing, and operational investment compared to broader macro questions last quarter.

  • Key metrics showed improvement: Q4 net income and EPS roughly tripled from the prior year, while loan growth, deposit growth, and core profitability strengthened.

  • Management's confidence level remained high; analysts maintained a constructive, detailed inquiry approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Nonaccrual loans increased, driven by SBA credits, though management described the uptick as manageable and aligned with industry trends.

  • The competitive deposit market and rate environment present ongoing funding and margin management challenges, especially as the Fed continues to cut rates.

  • Growth in provision expense remains tied to high loan growth, with CECL dynamics noted as a factor.

  • Management is investing in technology and AI to mitigate efficiency and scalability risks.

Final Takeaway

Live Oak Bancshares closed 2025 with record loan production, robust profitability, and significant progress in strategic initiatives such as business checking and Live Oak Express. Management emphasized operational improvements, deepening customer relationships, and strong credit management, while outlining an optimistic outlook for 2026 amid expected Fed cuts and ongoing investment in technology-driven growth and efficiency. The company aims for continued momentum, with an aspirational goal to achieve $1 billion in annual Live Oak Express production over time.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: 2026 ‘will show who’s actually delivering AI value’ – Scale AI CEO
data.description:
Scale AI CEO Jason Droege believes the AI industry is headed for a reckoning in 2026, with companies that fail to deliver real value being exposed.

“Next year in Davos, the wheat and the chaff are going to separate,” Droege said. “It will become very clear in the next twelve months who’s actually delivering value, what kind of use cases there are.”

Speaking in an interview with CNBC, Droege acknowledged that the current AI landscape is “definitely bubbly,” noting that many promises about what AI can do are simply not being delivered today.

He pointed to numerous well-funded companies and providers attempting to deliver value without succeeding. Once the separation occurs, Droege said, the industry can truly reassess where it stands.

Droege’s assessment comes from a position of strength, as Scale AI experienced significant growth in 2025. The company received a $14B+ investment from Meta and secured more than $1B in new bookings for the year, with more than half of that coming in the fourth quarter alone. Q4 was also Scale AI’s largest revenue quarter ever and its most profitable, underscoring the strong demand for AI infrastructure.

Scale AI’s approach to enterprise adoption centers on helping customers overcome the high bar required to implement AI reliably.

The company announced BP, Mayo Clinic, and Allianz as banner customers in Q4, deploying teams on-site to work with experienced model operators. Droege emphasized the importance of identifying the right problems – those that are technically solvable with AI rather than simply the hardest business challenges – and bringing together the right people, technology, and investments.

The company maintains an agnostic stance toward large language models, selecting whichever model best fits the customer’s application regardless of its Meta investment.

Scale AI has also expanded its work with the Department of War, evolving from data labeling five years ago to building applications that assist with operational planning and logistics – processes Droege described as highly manual and expensive, but well-suited for AI assistance.

AI and robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), (CHAT)

data.title: Old Republic cut to Neutral at Piper on commercial auto loss issues
data.description:
Piper Sandler downgraded Old Republic International (ORI) to Neutral from Overweight as the insurer struggles with commercial auto loss cost inflation.

Given that, Piper has become less optimistic about Old Republic's (ORI) specialty underwriting segment.

Analyst Paul Newsome recommends that investors avoid companies with loss reserve or loss trend issues. "We think companies that are entangled in commercial auto reserving or loss trend issues have less operational promise in a softening market than those who do not," he wrote in a note to clients.

Furthermore, "a trend cloud will likely cause the company to trade at a discount to peer average until the issue is proven solved," the analyst added.

In its Q4 earnings release, Old Republic (ORI) said its commercial auto initial 2025 accident-year loss ratio was increased by ~3 percentage points related to higher loss trends in the liability portion of its long-haul trucking case reserves that weren't yet reflected in paid claim data. Due to the catch-up for all four quarters, the provision led to a 12 pp increase in the Q4 loss ratio.

Piper trimmed its price target for ORI to $38 from $51.

Old Republic (ORI) stock slid 11% in Thursday midday trading.

The Neutral rating aligns with the SA Quant rating of Hold and diverges from the average Wall Street rating of Buy.
